file_name: Chiou et al. - 2024 - Multimodal image dataset for AI-based skin cancer (MIDAS) benchmarking.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper evaluated four state-of-the-art AI models (ADAE, HCI, ModelDerm, and DeepDerm) using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for dermatology tasks including melanoma classification, multi-class lesion categorization, and malignancy detection on the MIDAS dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper reports original experimental work involving the prospective enrollment of 796 patients to create the MIDAS dataset and the subsequent evaluation of four state-of-the-art AI models on this new dataset. It presents new results regarding model performance, generalizability, and dataset characteristics, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Multimodal Image Dataset for AI-based Skin Cancer (MIDAS) Benchmarking
brief_summary: This study introduces the Melanoma Research Alliance Multimodal Image Dataset for AI-based Skin Cancer (MIDAS), a prospectively recruited dataset containing paired dermoscopic and clinical images from 796 patients with biopsy-proven skin lesions. The authors evaluated four state-of-the-art AI models (ADAE, HCI, ModelDerm, and DeepDerm) on this diverse dataset to assess their real-world generalizability compared to previous benchmarks. The results demonstrated a significant reduction in performance for all AI models on MIDAS compared to their originally published metrics, highlighting challenges in generalizability. Additionally, the study found that algorithm performance was superior on dermoscopic images and clinical images taken at 15-cm distance versus 30-cm distance. These findings underscore the importance of rigorous external validation using multimodal, prospectively collected data to accurately assess the clinical viability of dermatology AI algorithms.

lesion_types_studied.details: 46 lesions (4.4% of total, 4.0% of surgically eligible melanocytic lesions). Evaluated as malignant by clinicians and models. Included in melanoma classification tasks (ADAE) and malignancy detection (ModelDerm, DeepDerm).
47 lesions (4.5% of total, 4.5% of surgically eligible melanocytic lesions). Grouped with invasive melanoma as 'malignant' for analysis. Included in melanoma classification tasks.
234 lesions (22% of total). Most common diagnosis (22.4%). Grouped as 'benign' for analysis. Included in multi-class classification (HCI) and melanoma vs non-melanoma tasks.
Second most common diagnosis (20.3%). Evaluated for clinician accuracy (81% sensitivity, 91% specificity for top-1 diagnosis). Included in HCI multi-class classification.

1.0% of total lesions. Includes angiomas, angiokeratomas, pyogenic granulomas, and hemorrhages. Included in HCI multi-class classification.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories including melanoma vs non-melanoma, malignant vs benign, and multi-class lesion types (e.g., melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, seborrheic keratosis).
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates four AI models (ADAE, HCI, ModelDerm, DeepDerm) for binary classification tasks (melanoma detection, malignancy detection) and multi-class classification tasks (lesion categorization). Performance metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC/AUROC, which are standard for classification tasks. ADAE was specifically designed for melanoma diagnosis (binary classification). HCI performed multi-class classification. ModelDerm and DeepDerm performed malignancy detection (binary classification).

datasets_used.name: MIDAS (Melanoma Research Alliance Multimodal Image Dataset for AI-based Skin Cancer)
datasets_used.description: A prospectively recruited, publicly available, paired dermoscopic- and clinical image-based dataset of biopsy-proven and dermatopathology-labeled skin lesions. Enrolled from two healthcare systems (Stanford University and Cleveland Clinic) between August 2020 and April 2023. Contains 796 patients, 1290 unique lesions, and 3830 total images (including dermoscopic, clinical at 15-cm and 30-cm distances, and virtual images). The primary analysis used 1046 biopsied lesions with histopathologic confirmation.

use.testing: Used as an external validation benchmark to evaluate the 'out-of-the-box' performance of four state-of-the-art AI models (ADAE, HCI, ModelDerm, DeepDerm) and compare against clinician diagnostic performance.
use.other: Publicly released for future multimodal AI development and benchmarking.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Calculation of confidence intervals for model performance metrics (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, AUC).
Training and optimization of the logistic regression-based meta-model for the proof-of-concept ensemble approach.
Optimization of regularization parameters for the logistic regression meta-model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for the comparisons of model performance or clinician accuracy. Performance metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals derived via bootstrapping, but no statistical tests comparing groups (e.g., model vs. clinician, or distance comparisons) with p-values are explicitly stated in the provided text.

ADAE.notes: Performance on melanocytic subset: Specificity 0.17 (95% CI: 0.13-0.22), Accuracy 0.38 (95% CI: 0.33-0.43).

HCI.notes: Original study reported 80.3% accuracy; 58.7% sensitivity and 93.3% specificity for malignant lesions.

ModelDerm.notes: Retrospective validation reported AUC 0.86 (95% CI: 0.85-0.88), sensitivity 0.63 (95% CI 0.59-0.65), specificity 0.90 (95% CI 0.89-0.90).

DeepDerm.notes: Original study reported 72.1% mean accuracy. Misclassification patterns: malignant lesions most commonly misclassified as non-neoplastic, followed by benign.

Clinicians.notes: Challenging subset: 27 lesions incorrectly classified by all four models at top-1 diagnosis.

DeepDerm_Distance_Subanalysis.notes: Accuracy and sensitivity improved for benign and malignant lesions with 15-cm photography compared to 30-cm. DeepDerm malignant sensitivity improved from 0.17 to 0.30 at 15cm.

architecture_used.main_model: Four state-of-the-art models were evaluated: ADAE (All Data are External), HCI (Human-Computer Interaction), ModelDerm, and DeepDerm.
architecture_used.pretraining: ADAE: Trained on ISIC 2020 challenge data (5-folds totaling 90 models). HCI: Fine-tuned on HAM10000 pigmented lesions dataset. ModelDerm: Trained on clinical images of skin lesions. DeepDerm: Trained on ISIC Dermoscopic Archive, Edinburgh Dermofit Library, and Stanford Medicine.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported for all models; ADAE and HCI used dermoscopic images. ModelDerm and DeepDerm used clinical images (at 15-cm and 30-cm distances). ADAE incorporated metadata fields (age, sex, anatomic location, width, height).

architecture_details.ADAE: Ensemble model comprising 18 separate CNN-based models (totaling 90 models across 5-folds). Four architectures incorporate metadata fields.
architecture_details.ModelDerm: Ensemble of ResNet variants. Consists of two modules: a lesion detector followed by a ResNet ensemble classifier. 2019 build version used.

training_details.ModelDerm: Trained on clinical images of skin lesions.
training_details.DeepDerm: Trained on comprehensive dataset including ISIC Dermoscopic Archive, Edinburgh Dermofit Library, and Stanford Medicine.
training_details.Proof-of-Concept Ensemble: Logistic regression meta-model optimized via grid search over regularization parameters with five-fold cross-validation. Weighted average model used equal or ADAE-prioritized weights.

classification_or_output_head.ADAE: Binary classification for melanoma (invasive melanoma and melanoma-in-situ). Thresholded for >95% sensitivity.
classification_or_output_head.HCI: Multi-class classification into 7 categories: akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc.
classification_or_output_head.ModelDerm: Binary malignancy detection. Malignancy score calculated as sum of malignant outputs + 0.2 × premalignant outputs.
classification_or_output_head.DeepDerm: Three-category classification (malignant, benign, non-neoplastic) and nine-category classification. Trained on 836-category taxonomy.
classification_or_output_head.Ensemble: Logistic regression meta-model or weighted average of ADAE and ModelDerm outputs.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proof-of-Concept Ensemble Models (Weighted Average and Logistic Regression Meta-Model)
preprocessing_techniques: Manual parsing of images to ensure image quality
Cleaning dataset by ensuring consistency across demographic labeling
Exclusion of images where ModelDerm was unable to detect a lesion (3% of images)
Alignment of pathology reports with DeepDerm taxonomy via consensus among dermatologists
conclusions: All four evaluated state-of-the-art AI models (ADAE, HCI, ModelDerm, and DeepDerm) demonstrated a significant reduction in performance on the MIDAS dataset compared to their originally published metrics, highlighting challenges in generalizability to real-world, diverse clinical populations.
Algorithm performance was superior on dermoscopic images compared to clinical images for malignancy detection tasks.
Clinical images taken at a 15-cm distance yielded higher accuracy and sensitivity for benign and malignant lesions compared to images taken at a 30-cm distance.
Dermatologists performing biopsies achieved 79% accuracy for top-1 diagnosis of malignant versus benign lesions, providing a comparative baseline for AI performance.
Algorithms trained primarily on pigmented lesion classification suffered performance declines when challenged with a broader disease distribution including non-melanocytic and inflammatory lesions.

The ADAE model maintained high sensitivity for melanoma (96.9%) but exhibited markedly low specificity (13.1%) on the MIDAS dataset, indicating its design prioritizes ruling out melanoma over ruling in specific diagnoses.
A subset of 27 lesions was identified as highly challenging, with all four AI models incorrectly classifying every tested image at the top-1 diagnosis level.
Ensemble approaches showed promising results, underscoring the potential benefit of multimodal strategies and the utility of the MIDAS dataset for refining such models.
The study acknowledges limitations including restricted access to some models, which constrained the exploration of fusion approaches and full utilization of the multimodal dataset.
important_remarks: All four evaluated state-of-the-art AI models (ADAE, HCI, ModelDerm, DeepDerm) showed significant performance reductions on the MIDAS dataset compared to their originally published metrics, highlighting generalizability challenges.

The ADAE model maintained high sensitivity for melanoma (96.9%) but exhibited markedly low specificity (13.1%) on the MIDAS dataset, indicating a design prioritizing ruling out melanoma over ruling in specific diagnoses.
Algorithm performance was superior on dermoscopic images compared to clinical images for malignancy detection tasks.
Clinical images taken at a 15-cm distance yielded higher accuracy and sensitivity for benign and malignant lesions compared to images taken at a 30-cm distance.
The dataset has a lower representation of patients with Fitzpatrick IV-VI skin tones (16.8% of the population), which may limit the assessment of algorithm performance in skin of color.
ModelDerm outperformed other algorithms on patients with darker Fitzpatrick skin types, potentially due to its training data being generated largely in South Korea.
The study is a preprint and has not been certified by peer review; it should not be used to guide clinical practice.
A subset of 27 lesions was identified as highly challenging, with all four AI models incorrectly classifying every tested image at the top-1 diagnosis level.
Ensemble approaches (combining dermoscopic and clinical inputs) showed promising results, with a logistic regression meta-model achieving an AUC of 0.86 and specificity of 0.96.
The study acknowledges limitations including restricted access to some models, which constrained the exploration of fusion approaches and full utilization of the multimodal dataset.
